title: Will it precipitate? Mixing calcium and fluoride solutions
scenario: Two clear solutions sit on the bench — **calcium nitrate**, Ca(NO3)2, and **sodium fluoride**, NaF. Neither holds a speck of solid; both are perfectly transparent. Pour 40.0 mL of 0.010 M Ca(NO3)2 into 60.0 mL of 0.010 M NaF and *does anything happen?* Calcium fluoride is the sparingly soluble pair here — $\mathrm{CaF_2(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca^{2+}(aq)} + 2\,\mathrm{F^-(aq)}$ — but nothing says a solid **must** appear. The test is the **reaction quotient**: mix, dilute each ion into the combined 100.0 mL, evaluate $Q = [\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}][\mathrm{F^-}]^2$ at that instant, and compare it to $K_{sp}$. If $Q > K_{sp}$ the solution is supersaturated and $\mathrm{CaF_2}$ crashes out; if $Q < K_{sp}$ it stays clear. No ICE table to solve — one comparison decides it.

assumptions.claim: Both source salts are soluble strong electrolytes that dissociate completely, and volumes are additive — so mixing 40.0 mL and 60.0 mL simply dilutes each ion into the combined 100.0 mL. The spectators $\mathrm{Na^+}$ and $\mathrm{NO_3^-}$ take no part.
Activities are approximated by molar concentrations (the ideal-dilute-solution model), which is why $Q$ and $K_{sp}$ are written with concentrations.
The thermodynamic prediction is taken to hold: $Q > K_{sp}$ means the solid will actually form, with no metastable supersaturation delaying it.

misconception.claim: Both solutions are clear and only 0.010 M — far too dilute for anything to precipitate when you pour them together.
